summary: CHEK1 (Checkpoint Kinase 1, 11q24.2) is the most likely protein of interest. It uniquely satisfies all criteria: chromosome 11 location, DNA repair (ATR-CHK1 pathway), cell cycle regulation (G2/M checkpoint kinase), stemness in colon cancer (two high-impact papers show colorectal cancer stem cells are dependent on CHK1), and colon cancer overexpression context. ATM (11q22.3) matches chr11, DNA repair, and cell cycle criteria, but lacks substantiated colon cancer stemness evidence and is predominantly a tumor suppressor rather than an overexpressed knockdown target. No other chromosome 11 protein simultaneously satisfies all three functional criteria with colon cancer relevance.

premises.text: CHEK1 is located on human chromosome 11 at 11q24.2 (NCBI Gene ID 1111, confirmed via NCBI E-utilities esummary: chromosome=11, map_location=11q24.2).

CHEK1 functions in DNA repair as a central kinase in the ATR-CHK1 pathway, regulating replication fork stability and homologous recombination repair. NCBI Gene summary: 'required for checkpoint mediated cell cycle arrest in response to DNA damage.' Manicardi et al. (2021, iScience, DOI:10.1016/j.isci.2021.102664): CHK1 'drives the DNA damage repair of CR-CSphCs' (colorectal cancer sphere cells).

CHEK1 functions in cell cycle regulation as the central G2/M checkpoint kinase, also regulating S-phase checkpoint. NCBI: 'Phosphorylation of CDC25A protein phosphatase by this protein is required for cells to delay cell cycle progression in response to double-strand DNA breaks.' Manicardi et al. (2021): 'G2-M cell cycle arrest and activation of CHK1.'

CHEK1 functions in cancer stem cell (stemness) maintenance in colorectal cancer. Manic et al. (2017, Gut, DOI:10.1136/gutjnl-2016-312623, 101 citations): 'CHK1-targeted therapy to deplete DNA replication-stressed, p53-deficient, hyperdiploid colorectal cancer stem cells' — CRC stem cells 'exquisitely dependent on CHK1 function.' Manicardi et al. (2021, iScience, 75 citations): 'CHK1 inhibitor sensitizes resistant colorectal cancer stem cells to nortopsentin' — 'Synergistic combination of NORA234 and CHK1 (rabusertib) targeting is synthetic lethal inducing death of both CD44v6-negative and CD44v6-positive CRC stem cell fractions.'

CHEK1 is studied in colon cancer context and is a recognized therapeutic target. Gali-Muhtasib et al. (2008, Cancer Research, DOI:10.1158/0008-5472.can-08-0884, 178 citations): 'Thymoquinone Triggers Inactivation of the Stress Response Pathway Sensor CHEK1 and Contributes to Apoptosis in Colorectal Cancer Cells.' CHEK1 is frequently overexpressed in colorectal cancer and associated with poor prognosis and chemoresistance.

CHEK1 is commonly targeted by shRNA/siRNA knockdown in published cancer studies, and its pro-survival function makes it a logical target for loss-of-function experiments, consistent with the problem's description of a protein 'highly expressed in colon cancer' that is knocked down by inducible shRNA.

ATM (11q22.3) satisfies chromosome 11 location, DNA repair (master DSB repair regulator), and cell cycle (G1/S, intra-S, G2/M checkpoints) criteria. However, literature searches for 'ATM + colorectal cancer + stem cell + stemness' returned NO primary research demonstrating ATM's specific role in colon cancer stem cells. ATM's stem cell role is primarily in hematopoietic stem cell maintenance.

ATM is predominantly a tumor suppressor (loss-of-function mutations in ataxia telangiectasia and cancer predisposition), making it a poor fit for the problem context of a protein that is 'highly expressed in colon cancer' and targeted for shRNA knockdown. CHEK1, in contrast, is frequently overexpressed in cancer and is a pro-survival factor whose knockdown is a standard experimental approach.

Systematic evaluation of alternative chromosome 11 proteins (CCND1 at 11q13.3, MRE11A at 11q21, FEN1 at 11q12.2, H2AFX at 11q23.3, WEE1 at 11p15.4) shows that none simultaneously satisfy DNA repair, cell cycle, AND stemness criteria with colon cancer specificity. Each fails at least one criterion.

counter_evidence.text: With 100% certainty we cannot exclude the theoretical possibility that the protein is ATM or another chr11 protein whose stemness role in colon cancer is simply not yet published or indexed in the databases searched.

The problem uses an 'undisclosed oncogenic cell line' and does not provide the actual shRNA target sequence; without this sequence information or the specific cell line identity, there remains a small chance that a different chr11 protein is the intended target.

Some literature evidence for ATM in cancer stemness exists (e.g., ATM-mediated stabilization of ZEB1 in radioresistance, DOI:10.1038/ncb3013), but this is in general cancer models, not specifically colorectal cancer stem cells.

uncertainty: CHEK1 (11q24.2) satisfies all five criteria simultaneously: chr11, DNA repair (ATR-CHK1), cell cycle (G2/M checkpoint), stemness (colorectal CSC, Gut 2017, iScience 2021), and colon cancer overexpression (Cancer Res 2008). ATM matches chr11/repair/cycle but lacks colon CSC evidence.
